messages.content:
This summary outlines the epidemiological factors and clinical context of insomnia, based on the provided text.

### **Summary: Epidemiology and Risk Factors of Insomnia**

#### **1. Definition and Prevalence**
*   **Definition:** Insomnia is characterized by dissatisfaction with the quantity or quality of sleep (difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, early morning awakening, or unrefreshing sleep).
*   **Prevalence:** It is a widespread condition, with approximately 10–15% of the general population suffering from chronic insomnia and up to 30% experiencing occasional or transient symptoms **(p. 11, p. 17)**. 
*   **Diagnosis:** Estimates vary significantly (from 3.9% to 22.1%) depending on the diagnostic criteria used, such as DSM-IV, ICD-10, or ICSD-2 **(p. 18)**.

#### **2. Socio-Demographic Risk Factors**
*   **Gender:** Women are at a higher risk of developing insomnia than men, with a reported ratio of approximately 4:1 for symptoms. This is potentially linked to hormonal changes (menopause) and higher rates of comorbid anxiety and depression **(p. 19, p. 20)**.
*   **Age:** Prevalence often increases with age, though some researchers argue this is due to associated physical/mental morbidities and life changes (e.g., retirement) rather than aging itself **(p. 20)**.
*   **Marital & Social Status:** Being separated, divorced, or widowed is consistently associated with higher rates of insomnia **(p. 20)**.
*   **Socio-economic Factors:** Lower income and education levels are often linked to insomnia, likely functioning as a proxy for social disadvantages like unemployment or poor living conditions **(p. 21)**.

#### **3. Morbidity and Substance Use**
*   **Physical Health:** Conditions involving pain or distress (e.g., fibromyalgia, arthritis, heart disease, and digestive disorders) are strongly and independently associated with insomnia **(p. 21)**.
*   **Mental Health:** Insomnia has a **bidirectional relationship** with mental health. It acts as both a risk factor for and a consequence of depression and anxiety. Up to 50% of insomnia cases may be secondary to an underlying psychiatric disorder **(p. 21, p. 22)**.
*   **Substances:** 
    *   **Alcohol:** While initially sedating, alcohol impairs sleep architecture and increases arousal later in the night **(p. 23)**.
    *   **Other Factors:** Caffeine, stimulants, nicotine, and drug withdrawal are significant contributors to sleep disruption **(p. 23)**.

#### **Clinical Importance**
Despite the high prevalence and the associated risks—such as increased work errors, accidents, immune dysfunction, and mental health decline—insomnia remains largely under-recognized and under-treated in clinical practice **(p. 11)**.

Understanding Insomnia: Causes and Therapy
  • Insomnia: Inability to fall or stay asleep (p. 11).
  • Affects up to 15% of the general population (p. 11).

This text provides the front matter and publication details for the academic book *Can’t Sleep? Issues of Being an Insomniac*, edited by Dr. Saddichha Sahoo and published by IntechOpen in 2012.

Key Information Points

Purpose and Scope
The book serves as a compendium of expert contributions regarding the challenges, science, and clinical aspects of insomnia. It features research from an international group of contributors, including experts from various medical and scientific institutions.

Publication Details
The work was published in Croatia under the IntechOpen imprint. It is available in both print and electronic formats, with specific ISBNs provided for the hard copy and the eBook version (ISBN 978-953-51-0261-8 and ISBN 978-953-51-6889-8, respectively). The text is indexed in the Web of Science Core Collection.

Legal and Licensing Information
The book is governed by a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License for individual chapters, allowing for distribution and reproduction provided the original authors and the source are clearly cited. However, the compilation as a whole remains under the copyright of IntechOpen. The publisher disclaims liability for the clinical accuracy or consequences arising from the information provided within the chapters.

Editor Credentials
Dr. Saddichha Sahoo is a psychiatrist and researcher with expertise in addiction psychiatry and psychopharmacology. He is currently affiliated with the University of British Columbia and has an extensive academic background, including over 75 scientific publications and prior editorial experience.

This book provides a comprehensive overview of insomnia, organized into three primary sections that bridge epidemiological data, clinical practice, and psychopharmacological research.

Part 1: Epidemiology and Risk Factors
This section establishes the foundational landscape of sleep disorders. It examines the prevalence and contributing risk factors of insomnia at a population level. Additionally, it explores the pharmacological dimension of the condition, specifically detailing how certain medications can induce insomnia as a secondary adverse effect.

Part 2: Clinical Issues, Diagnosis, and Management
This part shifts focus to the clinical evaluation of patients. It introduces standardized tools for measuring quality of life in those with sleep disorders and provides a clinical framework for addressing fatigue. The section culminates in a practical guide to the diagnosis and medical management of insomnia.

Part 3: Psychopharmacology of Insomnia
The final section addresses medicinal chemistry and therapeutic strategy. It discusses the complexities of treating insomnia in patients who also suffer from comorbid mental health conditions. Furthermore, it includes a technical analysis regarding the physical and chemical properties of barbiturates, utilizing the Randić descriptor to conduct a structural relationship study on the behavior of these molecules, specifically concerning the octanol-water partition coefficient, denoted as $P$:

$$\log P = \dots$$

This technical analysis highlights the relationship between molecular structure and the biodegradation of sedative substances.

### Summary of the Preface on Insomnia

The preface provides a foundational overview of insomnia, defining it as a psychosomatic disorder involving dissatisfaction with sleep quality or quantity. Key points include:

**Definition and Clinical Presentation**
Insomnia is characterized by the inability to fall asleep, frequent nocturnal awakenings, early morning awakening, or non-restorative sleep. It was first described as a psychosomatic condition by Johann Heinroth in 1818.

**Epidemiology and Prevalence**
* The condition is highly prevalent, with 33–50% of the adult population reporting symptoms and approximately 10–15% meeting diagnostic thresholds.
* Prevalence is higher among women, older adults, the widowed or divorced, and individuals with co-morbid psychiatric or medical conditions.
* Despite its ubiquity, insomnia remains significantly under-recognized and under-treated.

**Impact and Risks**
Chronic insomnia is a serious, complex condition linked to daytime impairment in cognition, mood, and performance. The societal and individual consequences are severe, including increased absenteeism, higher rates of workplace errors, fatal road accidents, and a noted correlation with anxiety, depression, substance abuse, and immune system dysfunction.

**Scope of the Book**
The text functions as a comprehensive resource for clinicians and researchers, covering:
* Epidemiology and risk factors.
* Clinician errors in diagnosis and the role of medication-induced insomnia.
* The impact of insomnia on quality of life and productivity.
* Clinical management strategies and a review of pharmacological interventions, specifically barbiturates.

### Summary: Epidemiology and Risk Factors of Insomnia

This text explores the epidemiology, prevalence, and multifactorial risk profile of insomnia. 

**Prevalence and Diagnostic Challenges**
Insomnia is a major health issue, with roughly 10–15% of the general population suffering regularly and up to 35% experiencing transient symptoms. Accurate prevalence estimation is hindered by inconsistent diagnostic criteria across international manuals (ICSD-2, DSM-IV-TR, and ICD-10). Depending on the methodology and definitions used, prevalence rates vary widely from 3.9% to 40%. Generally, researchers estimate that specific insomnia disorders affect 5–10% of the population, while symptoms occur in approximately 30%.

**Socio-demographic and Economic Risk Factors**
*   **Gender:** Women report higher rates of insomnia than men, with a reported risk ratio of 1.41 ($95\% CI 1.28–1.55$). Hormonal fluctuations during menopause, higher rates of comorbid anxiety/depression, and potential social differences in emotional expression are suggested drivers.
*   **Age:** Prevalence often increases with age, though some researchers argue this is due to associated physical health decline and life stressors—such as retirement or bereavement—rather than biological aging itself. Conversely, younger groups may experience initial insomnia linked to lifestyle and circadian factors.
*   **Marital and Socio-economic Status:** Marital separation, divorce, and widowhood are consistently linked to higher insomnia risks. While low income and literacy often correlate with higher prevalence, these links frequently disappear when adjusted for other social disadvantages and comorbidities.
*   **Race/Ethnicity:** Data remains mixed; some studies suggest higher incidence among black populations, particularly black women, while others (such as the study in Bambuí, Brazil) find no statistically significant difference after controlling for variables.

**Physical and Mental Morbidity**
There is a profound, bidirectional relationship between insomnia and overall health:

1.  **Physical Health:** Chronic conditions involving pain or distress (e.g., fibromyalgia, arthritis, back pain, and gastrointestinal disorders) show an independent association with insomnia.
2.  **Mental Health:** Psychiatric conditions are among the strongest correlates of insomnia. Insomnia often acts as both a precursor and a consequence of mood and anxiety disorders. Patients reporting insomnia symptoms exhibit significantly higher relative risks for depression (up to 9.0) and generalized anxiety (up to 3.9) compared to healthy sleepers. 

Ultimately, the text emphasizes that insomnia is a complex, reciprocal condition best understood through a biopsychosocial lens, where physical ailments, emotional states, and socio-economic contexts continuously interact to drive the development and persistence of sleep loss.

This section explores various risk factors for insomnia, highlighting the complex, often bidirectional, relationship between sleep disruption and physical, hormonal, and psychosocial stressors.

**Substance Use and Lifestyle Factors**
Alcohol, while initially sedating, negatively impacts the sleep cycle and becomes less effective with continued use. Frequent heavy drinking and the consumption of caffeine are strongly linked to insomnia. Inconsistent findings regarding illicit drugs, such as cannabis, also suggest associations with frequent sleep disruption. Furthermore, tobacco use is positively correlated with increased sleep latency and daytime sleepiness.

**Chronic Pain**
Chronic pain and insomnia share a reciprocal relationship; pain contributes to sleep difficulty, and sleep loss often exacerbates the perception of pain. Data from the Israel National Health Survey indicate that chronic pain is associated with both sleep complaints and higher health care usage, even in the absence of psychiatric comorbidities.

**Menopause**
Insomnia prevalence significantly increases during the transition from premenopause to postmenopause. This is largely driven by hormone fluctuations and vasomotor symptoms like hot flashes and night sweats. While some studies suggest hormone replacement therapy (HRT) may improve sleep, results remain inconsistent, likely due to varying treatment protocols and individual subject characteristics.

**Psychosocial Factors and Stress**
Stress is a primary driver of both the onset and persistence of insomnia. Exposure to stressful life events (SLE) — such as personal losses, health crises, or relationship conflicts — often triggers sleep difficulties. Over time, sleep loss itself becomes a stressor, creating a detrimental feedback loop.

**Workplace Stress Models**
Two primary models explain the link between occupation and sleep:

1. The Demand-Control Model: High psychological demands coupled with low autonomy (control) create "job strain," which is significantly associated with sleep disturbances. Social support acts as a moderator in this relationship.
2. The Effort-Reward Imbalance (ERI) Model: This model posits that an imbalance between high work effort and low rewards (salary, esteem, promotion prospects) causes stress, leading to sleep deprivation. "Over-commitment" to work is a significant predictor of insomnia, often more so than work demands alone.

Evidence suggests that these stressors increase the risk of sleep disruption, while high levels of social support and adequate work rewards may facilitate recovery from insomnia. Integrating both the Demand-Control and ERI models is considered the most effective approach for identifying workers at high risk for sleep disorders.

This summary covers the provided sections on clinical issues and fatigue (pages 39–48), focusing on the impact of sleep disorders on daily life and the nature of fatigue.

Clinical Measurement of Sleep Disorders (Chapter 3)
Sleep disorders are not merely nocturnal issues; they significantly impact the daytime functioning and the overall health-related quality of life (HRQoL) of patients. Clinicians utilize specific quality-of-life assessment tools to evaluate the burden of insomnia effectively. These measures are essential because they capture the patient's subjective experience—such as mood, social performance, and cognitive ability—which objective physiological tests alone may overlook. Assessing HRQoL allows medical professionals to develop more comprehensive management plans that address both sleep latency and the resultant daytime impairment.

Understanding Fatigue (Chapter 4)
Fatigue is a central clinical concern for individuals suffering from insomnia. It represents a state of exhaustion that persists despite attempts to rest. Unlike simple sleepiness, fatigue is a complex, multidimensional state that affects physical, mental, and emotional endurance. 

Key clinical considerations regarding fatigue include:
* The distinction between sleepiness (the tendency to fall asleep) and fatigue (a lack of energy or motivation).
* The connection between chronic lack of sleep and the disruption of normal circadian rhythms, which leads to cumulative fatigue.
* The societal impact, including decreased workplace productivity, increased error rates, and a higher risk of motor vehicle accidents.
* The reciprocal relationship between fatigue and mental health, where persistent fatigue often exacerbates underlying anxiety or depressive symptoms.

Conclusion
Effective management of insomnia requires acknowledging that sleep disturbance is a systemic issue. By integrating HRQoL metrics and addressing the debilitating effects of chronic fatigue, clinicians can improve outcomes for patients who struggle with the physical and psychological consequences of being an insomniac.

The section covering pages 49–64 (Chapter 4 and the beginning of Chapter 5) focuses on the clinical understanding of fatigue and the diagnostic management of insomnia.

**Fatigue**
The text distinguishes fatigue as a significant clinical concern often associated with sleep disorders. It functions as a subjective sensation of tiredness, exhaustion, or lack of energy that impairs physical and mental function. In the context of sleep medicine, fatigue is frequently analyzed as both a symptom of underlying pathology and a consequence of chronic sleep deprivation. Evaluation requires clinicians to differentiate between primary fatigue and fatigue secondary to co-morbid medical or psychiatric conditions.

**Diagnosis of Insomnia**
The diagnosis of insomnia is primarily based on clinical assessment rather than objective laboratory testing. Key diagnostic criteria include:
- A subjective report of difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, or experiencing early morning awakenings.
- The presence of adequate opportunity for sleep, ruling out external circumstances (e.g., environmental noise or shift work).
- Associated daytime impairment, such as reduced cognitive performance, mood disturbances, or physical exhaustion.

**Management Strategies**
The management of insomnia involves a multi-faceted approach:
1. Identification and treatment of underlying comorbidities, which are present in a significant portion of patients.
2. Behavioral interventions, which serve as the first-line treatment for chronic cases.
3. Pharmacological intervention, where medications are selected based on the patient's specific insomnia profile and clinical history.

The text emphasizes that because chronic insomnia leads to long-term societal and individual consequences—including increased accident risk and psychiatric morbidity—clinicians must move beyond "under-treating" the condition and establish rigorous diagnostic pathways to improve patient outcomes and quality of life.

### Summary: The Diagnosis and Treatment of Insomnia 

Based on the provided text (Chapter 5), the clinical approach to insomnia focuses on systematic evaluation and evidence-based management.

**Core Clinical Considerations**
Insomnia is clinically defined as a subjective dissatisfaction with the quantity or quality of sleep. It manifests through several primary complaints:
* Difficulty initiating sleep despite being in bed.
* Frequent nighttime awakenings with difficulty returning to sleep.
* Early morning awakenings.
* Non-restorative sleep resulting in daytime impairment.

**Epidemiological Context**
The condition is highly prevalent, affecting 33-50% of the adult population to some degree, with 10% suffering from chronic insomnia. Chronic insomnia is characterized by ongoing cognitive, mood, and performance impairments. Beyond personal distress, this condition carries significant public health risks, including increased hospital utilization, workplace absenteeism, occupational errors, and higher rates of fatal road accidents. There is also a strong correlation between chronic insomnia and other health issues, such as depression, anxiety, substance use, and immune dysfunction.

**Diagnostic and Management Challenges**
Despite its prevalence, insomnia is frequently under-recognized and under-treated. Clinicians must distinguish between transient (acute) and chronic forms, as the latter requires more rigorous intervention. Effective management involves:
* Assessing individual and societal risks during patient evaluation.
* Identifying comorbid psychiatric or medical illnesses, which are present in approximately 40% of chronic cases.
* Avoiding diagnostic errors by identifying medications that may induce or exacerbate sleep disturbances.
* Integrating both pharmacological and non-pharmacological approaches to improve patient quality of life and productivity.

**Pharmacological Overview**
The text notes the role of psychopharmacology, specifically highlighting the historical and clinical use of barbiturates in the management of insomnia. The structural relationship of such substances is often analyzed using chemical descriptors to evaluate properties like their octanol-water partition coefficient, expressed as $\log P$, which helps in understanding their behavior and biodegradation within the body.

### Summary: Psychopharmacology of Insomnia (pp. 81–98)

This section focuses on the clinical management of insomnia, particularly when it presents alongside other mental health conditions, and examines the chemical properties of historical sedative medications.

#### 1. Treatment of Insomnia with Comorbid Mental Illness
Insomnia is frequently co-morbid with various psychiatric disorders, including depression, anxiety, and substance use issues. The chapter emphasizes that:
*   Effective management requires addressing the underlying psychiatric illness alongside the sleep disorder.
*   Clinicians must be cautious of the bidirectional relationship: sleep disturbances can exacerbate mental health symptoms, while psychiatric conditions often act as drivers for chronic insomnia.
*   The clinical approach should prioritize patient safety, considering the risks of potential dependency or interference with medications used to treat primary mental illnesses.

#### 2. Structural Relationships and Barbiturates
The text shifts to a technical examination of barbiturates, a class of drugs historically utilized for sedation. Key technical points include:
*   The study utilizes the Randić descriptor to analyze the structural relationship between octanol-water partition coefficients and the biodegradation of barbiturate molecules.
*   This quantitative analysis helps understand the environmental and physiological breakdown of these substances. 
*   The study relies on mathematical modeling, specifically looking at the correlation between molecular structure and properties such as the octanol-water partition coefficient ($K_{ow}$), which is represented conceptually in the study of lipophilicity and drug behavior.
*   The research aims to provide a deeper understanding of the chemical characteristics of these medicines, which is essential for understanding their efficacy and safety profiles in clinical practice.

#### Key Takeaways
*   Clinical oversight is vital when pharmacological interventions are used, as insomnia is rarely an isolated condition in patients with psychiatric comorbidities.
*   Understanding the molecular structure and degradation pathways (such as those of barbiturates) is an essential component of professional psychopharmacology, ensuring that the substances used to induce sleep are understood in terms of their long-term biological and chemical impact.

This summary covers the provided content, including the book preface and the findings regarding the epidemiology and risk factors of insomnia.

### Overview of Insomnia
Insomnia, derived from the Latin "in" (no) and "somnus" (sleep), is a psychosomatic disorder defined by a subjective dissatisfaction with the quantity or quality of sleep. Symptoms include difficulty falling asleep, frequent nocturnal awakenings, early morning waking, or unrefreshing sleep. It is highly prevalent, affecting 33–50% of the adult population to some degree, with 10–15% suffering from chronic insomnia.

### Societal and Health Impacts
Chronic insomnia is often under-diagnosed and under-treated. It results in significant impairments to cognition, mood, and daily performance. The societal costs include increased absenteeism, decreased productivity, and a higher rate of accidents, including fatal road accidents. Serious health risks associated with chronic insomnia include:
* Development of depression and anxiety.
* Increased risk of substance abuse and suicidal ideation.
* Possible immune system dysfunction.

### Epidemiology and Risk Factors
Research indicates that insomnia is more common in specific demographic groups, including:
* Women.
* Older individuals.
* Individuals who are divorced, separated, or widowed.
* Populations with co-morbid medical or psychiatric conditions.

### Diagnostic Challenges and Complexity
A major hurdle in addressing insomnia is the lack of a universal, standardized definition or classification system. Because symptoms often fluctuate, prevalence estimates vary significantly depending on the criteria used. Clinical research suggests a reciprocal relationship between insomnia and psychosocial or medical conditions, where each can exacerbate the other. This complexities necessitates that clinicians remain vigilant regarding the individual and societal risks linked to the disorder.

### Scientific Context
The book provides a comprehensive, multi-disciplinary look at these issues, ranging from pharmacological treatments like barbiturates to the study of molecular properties in medicines. For example, some chapters analyze the physical chemistry of substances, such as the modeling of octanol-water partitioning coefficients for drugs, identifying trends through the use of specific descriptors:
$$D = \sum a_i^2$$
(Note: Specific mathematical models are further explored in Chapter 7 regarding the biodegradation of sedative medicines).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: Why do prevalence estimates for insomnia vary significantly across different studies?
options: Because insomnia is exclusively a subjective experience that cannot be measured by scientific tools.
Because the studies focus only on specific geographical locations with limited demographic diversity.
Because researchers use inconsistent definitions, diagnostic criteria, and assessment methodologies.
Because insomnia is a rapidly evolving disorder that changes its diagnostic criteria annually.
questions.explanation: The text highlights that variation in prevalence estimates stems from inconsistent definitions (such as those of the DSM-IV, ICD-10, and RDC/ICSD-2), different diagnostic criteria, and the use of varying measurement instruments.

questions.question: What is the relationship between insomnia and other medical or psychiatric conditions?
options: Insomnia is strictly a primary condition and is never secondary to other health issues.
There is no evidence suggesting a link between insomnia and psychiatric comorbidities.
The relationship is considered reciprocal, as each condition may cause, maintain, or exacerbate the other.
The relationship is generally unidirectional, where insomnia causes medical issues but is not caused by them.
questions.explanation: The text states that the relationship between insomnia and psychosocial/medical conditions is believed to be reciprocal, meaning they can influence and worsen each other.

questions.question: Based on the text, what is a potential reason for the higher prevalence of insomnia in women compared to men?
options: Women consistently have lower socioeconomic status than men globally.
Men are genetically immune to the effects of hormonal changes on sleep quality.
Women have higher prevalences of anxiety and depression, which are linked to insomnia.
Women are biologically unable to enter deep sleep stages.
questions.explanation: The text notes that women present a higher prevalence of mental disorders, especially depression and anxiety, which increases their risk of insomnia, alongside potential hormonal factors.

questions.question: How should clinicians interpret the association between insomnia and the aging process?
options: The elderly suffer from insomnia solely due to retirement and loss of family members.
Insomnia is an inevitable, mandatory component of the natural aging process.
Insomnia is actually rare in elderly populations compared to younger demographics.
Insomnia should not be considered an inherent part of aging, but rather a result of multifactorial causes like comorbidities.
questions.explanation: While insomnia prevalence increases with age, the text suggests it should not be considered an inherent component of aging, but rather a result of multifactorial issues like increased medical problems and medication use.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the potential impact of chronic insomnia on individuals and society?
options: It is a temporary condition that clears up on its own without requiring clinical recognition.
It is associated with increased accidents at work, higher healthcare utilization, and potential immune dysfunction.
It leads to improved performance due to 'hyper-alertness' caused by sleep deprivation.
It primarily causes minor fatigue with no measurable impact on professional productivity.
questions.explanation: The text explains that chronic insomnia leads to daytime impairments that affect the individual's performance, health, and safety, resulting in increased hospital visits, work accidents, and risks like immune dysfunction.

cards.front: What is the etymology of the word 'insomnia'?
cards.back: It originates from the Latin 'in' (no) and 'somnus' (sleep).

cards.front: What are the common clinical presentations of insomnia?
cards.back: Difficulty falling asleep, waking up multiple times with trouble returning to sleep, early morning awakening, or unrefreshing sleep.

cards.front: Approximately what percentage of the general population suffers from chronic insomnia?
What is the primary difference in prevalence estimates between DSM-IV-TR and ICD-10 criteria for insomnia?
cards.back: Prevalence estimates vary significantly; for example, one study found 22.1% using DSM-IV-TR versus 3.9% using ICD-10 criteria.

cards.front: Compared to men, what is the relative risk for women in developing insomnia?
cards.back: Women have a 41% higher risk of developing insomnia compared to men.

cards.front: What hormonal changes are linked to higher insomnia prevalence in women?
cards.back: A decrease in circulating estrogens and progesterone, particularly related to menopause.

cards.front: How do mental disorders influence the development of insomnia in women?
cards.back: Women have a higher prevalence of depression and anxiety, which are conditions that increase the risk of insomnia.

cards.front: Why is the relationship between insomnia and comorbid medical/psychological conditions described as 'reciprocal'?
cards.back: Each condition may cause, maintain, or exacerbate the other.

cards.front: What is a major consequence of chronic insomnia in the workplace?
cards.back: Increased absenteeism, errors in work, and increased risk of accidents.

cards.front: Beyond age itself, what factors often contribute to sleep decline in the elderly?
cards.back: Life changes such as retirement or loss of family members, as well as an increase in circulatory, digestive, and respiratory diseases.

slides.title: Understanding Insomnia: Causes and Therapy
bullets: Insomnia: Inability to fall or stay asleep (p. 11).
Affects up to 15% of the general population (p. 11).
slides.notes: Insomnia is a prevalent psychosomatic disorder impacting quality of life. This session explores its origins and clinical management.

slides.title: Epidemiology and Risk Factors
bullets: Symptoms common in 33-50% of the population (p. 11).
Chronic insomnia linked to psychiatric morbidity (p. 11).
Risk factors: age, female gender, and co-morbid illness (p. 11).
slides.notes: Insomnia frequently coexists with medical and mental health issues, often creating a cycle that exacerbates both conditions.

slides.title: Drugs Inducing Insomnia
bullets: Medications can disrupt sleep via various mechanisms (p. 25).
Beta-blockers and NSAIDs affect melatonin production (p. 25).
Stimulants increase dopaminergic and noradrenergic activity (p. 25).
Withdrawal from benzodiazepines or barbiturates causes insomnia (p. 25).
slides.notes: Clinicians must be aware that pharmacological treatments for other conditions may inadvertently induce or worsen sleep architecture.

slides.title: Clinical Consequences
bullets: Increased workplace errors and industrial accidents (p. 11).
Higher risk of depression, anxiety, and suicide (p. 11).
slides.notes: Chronic insomnia extends beyond the night, posing significant risks to individual productivity, safety, and psychological well-being.
